skill.name: browser-use
skill.description: Automates browser workflows through Playwright MCP, including navigation, form interaction, screenshots, accessibility snapshots, and page data extraction.
skill.summary: Run Playwright MCP browser automation tasks from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: AbdullahMalik17

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-risk matches, but most weak-crypto and backtick findings are false positives from markdown examples and JSON schema snippets. Confirmed risks are legitimate but sensitive browser automation behaviors: shell-based stdio MCP startup, unpinned npx package execution, arbitrary browser code execution, networked MCP calls, and local process helpers. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential theft, covert exfiltration, or confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Stdio Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The MCP client can launch a stdio server command through subprocess.Popen with shell=True. This is useful for MCP workflows, but any untrusted value passed to --stdio can execute arbitrary shell syntax.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly passes the --stdio command string to subprocess.Popen with shell=True. The behavior is intentional, but the command source is user controlled.

medium_findings.title: Unpinned Network Package Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill starts Playwright MCP with npx @playwright/mcp@latest. Running the latest package from the network creates supply chain and reproducibility risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both the documentation and helper script invoke @playwright/mcp@latest. This is a concrete external package execution pattern, though it targets a known Playwright package.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Browser JavaScript Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill documents browser_evaluate and browser_run_code, which execute JavaScript or Playwright code in the active browser page. This can read page state or perform web actions if used on sensitive sites.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced tools explicitly accept JavaScript or Playwright code strings. This is an expected capability for browser automation, but it has meaningful security impact.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Browser Actions and Form Submission
medium_findings.description: The tool set supports navigation, clicks, typing, form filling, screenshots, and browser close actions. These actions are legitimate but can submit data or alter web sessions if used without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation lists destructive browser operations and form interaction parameters. The risk is capability based, not evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: User-Controlled HTTP Endpoint and Headers
low_findings.description: The MCP client accepts a URL and optional headers, then sends JSON-RPC requests to that endpoint. No hardcoded exfiltration endpoint was found, but users should avoid sending secrets to untrusted MCP servers.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network calls and custom headers are clearly present. The examples target localhost and I did not find evidence of covert data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Predictable Temporary PID File and Process Termination
low_findings.description: The helper scripts store a PID file under /tmp and stop processes with kill, kill -9, or pkill patterns. This is ordinary local process management, but predictable temporary files and broad process matching can affect unrelated local processes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts explicitly use /tmp PID files and process termination commands. The behavior is local and task related, so the severity remains low.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto and Backtick Matches Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The many weak-crypto and Ruby backtick detections in the Playwright reference are markdown tool schemas and fenced examples, not executable cryptographic code or Ruby shell execution. No evidence found of weak cryptography being used by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown code fences, JSON schema fragments, or command examples. They do not implement cryptography or Ruby command execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The stdio transport launches a shell command string. This should only be used with trusted commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact subprocess.Popen call uses shell=True with a command string stored from CLI input.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Package Execution Using latest
dangerous_patterns.description: The server startup path runs an unpinned package version from npm via npx.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command text includes @latest in both the executable helper and documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Code Execution Tools
dangerous_patterns.description: The tool reference includes browser_evaluate and browser_run_code for executing browser-side code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented parameters require JavaScript or Playwright code strings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Process Kill Helpers
dangerous_patterns.description: The stop helper uses kill, kill -9, and pkill to stop Playwright MCP processes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present and tied to local cleanup. The matching pattern lowers confidence that unrelated processes will be affected.

content.user_title: Automate Browser Tasks with Playwright MCP
content.value_statement: Manual browser work is slow and hard to repeat across pages, forms, and test flows. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured Playwright MCP commands for controlled browser automation.
actual_capabilities: Start and stop a local Playwright MCP server for browser sessions.
Navigate pages, go back, and wait for text or timing conditions.
Capture accessibility snapshots and screenshots for page inspection.
Click elements, type text, fill forms, and select dropdown options by snapshot reference.
Run page JavaScript or Playwright snippets for advanced extraction and workflows.
Emit MCP tool documentation from a running server for reference updates.
limitations: Requires Node.js, npx, Python 3, and Playwright browser dependencies.
Runs browser actions through a local MCP server that must be started first.
Can interact with real websites, so user review is needed before submissions or purchases.
Uses @playwright/mcp@latest by default, so package behavior can change over time.

use_cases.title: Verify Web Forms
use_cases.description: Exercise signup, checkout, or admin forms and capture the resulting page state for review.

use_cases.title: Collect Page Evidence
use_cases.description: Open competitor or customer-facing pages, capture screenshots, and summarize visible content.

use_cases.title: Automate Research Workflows
use_cases.description: Navigate public web pages, extract visible facts, and repeat the same steps across similar sites.

prompt_templates.title: Open a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Start the browser MCP server, open the target URL I provide, take an accessibility snapshot, and tell me what major sections are visible.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Navigate to this page, wait until the main content loads, capture a full-page screenshot, and summarize the visible content with source context.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Form Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use browser snapshots to identify the form fields, fill them with the test values I provide, stop before final submission, and ask for confirmation.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Structured Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a browser workflow across the listed URLs, extract the requested visible fields, capture evidence screenshots, and report errors or blocked pages.

output_examples.input: Open a product page and summarize what is visible.
output_examples.output: The page loaded successfully. The visible content includes product name, price, feature list, customer rating, and a primary purchase button.

output_examples.input: Check whether the login form has email and password fields.
Capture evidence after navigating a support article.
output_examples.output: A full-page screenshot was captured. The article title, updated date, table of contents, and troubleshooting steps were visible.

best_practices: Start with browser_snapshot before clicking or typing so element references are current.
Ask for confirmation before submitting forms, making purchases, or changing account settings.
Pin the Playwright MCP package version when using this skill in repeatable production workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not pass untrusted shell text to the stdio MCP command option.
Do not run browser code on pages that contain secrets unless the user explicitly approves it.
Do not rely on screenshots alone for actions when accessibility snapshots provide safer element references.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser navigation, page inspection, screenshots, form interaction, and selected Playwright MCP tool calls.

faq.question: Does it require a local server?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill starts a local Playwright MCP server and sends browser commands to that server.

faq.question: Can it submit forms?
faq.answer: Yes. It can fill and submit forms, so users should confirm before any real submission.

faq.question: Can it scrape websites?
faq.answer: It can extract visible page data, but users must follow site terms, privacy rules, and applicable laws.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: It runs local commands, starts networked tooling, and controls a browser, but no malicious intent was found.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
